children.description:
for an autocomplete UI that feels instant, you want to cut the time to first useful suggestion as much as possible. with cloud llm providers (anthropic, openai, etc.) you can’t avoid some network + model latency, so real-time tricks matter more than chasing a few ms of model fps. here’s what actually moves the needle for this kind of task:  ￼

use a lighter model or smaller endpoint
pick the fastest model that gives acceptable quality. smaller models finish faster per token, and for autocomplete you don’t need deep reasoning — you just need plausible next tokens. smaller models can be 2-5× faster than big ones.  ￼

stream partial suggestions and render them as soon as they arrive
even if you can’t produce fully structured lists mid-stream, start showing the first tokens/text chunks immediately with the streaming API (e.g., anthropric/claude messages.stream). that shifts perceived latency drastically because the user sees suggestions while the model is still generating.  ￼

limit the amount the model needs to generate
constrain max tokens aggressively, and frame your prompt so the output is as short as possible (for example, “return three comma-separated suggestions without extra text”). less text = fewer tokens = less time.  ￼

cache/“warm up” repeated contexts
if you expect many similar queries (like in an editor or chat UI), either cache recent completions or reuse prepended context so the model doesn’t need to re-read long histories. for some workloads computing prompt state once and reusing cached embeddings or hidden states can cut latency.  ￼

think about semantic caching of whole completions
if the user patterns are predictable (e.g., common code snippets), you can keep a small semantic index of old queries + replies and serve them instantly if the new input is close enough. this can avoid llm calls entirely for a lot of autocomplete traffic.  ￼

pre-compute responses for expected prefixes
if you can anticipate common hotkey patterns (like next word after certain tokens), pre-generate suggestions while the user types (in a background worker). that spreads latency out so when they hit the hotkey, you already have something to show.

keep networking tight
run your backend as close to your users as possible, use persistent connections, and tune HTTP keep-alive — the round-trip overhead often dwarfs the actual token generation in cloud APIs.

in practice for a 3-item suggestion feature:
• call the model with streaming turned on so you can append tokens live.
• set prompt + stops so generation is minimal (just the list).
• choose the fastest model that still works for your domain.
• cache similar prefixes and reuse those completions when possible.

that combo will give you the lowest perceived latency for hotkey autocomplete. real instant (<50 ms) usually means some component runs locally or via a tiny distilled model, but for cloud APIs these steps get you as close as you can without custom inference hardware.  ￼
